content.explanation:
BEM (Block Element Modifier) is a CSS naming methodology developed by Yandex that provides a structured, predictable way to name CSS classes. It solves the fundamental challenge of CSS at scale: as stylesheets grow, class names collide, specificity wars erupt, and developers cannot safely modify styles without risking breakage elsewhere. BEM addresses these problems through a strict naming convention that makes the relationship between HTML structure and CSS rules immediately visible.

The BEM naming pattern follows the format: block__element--modifier. A Block is a standalone, reusable component that is meaningful on its own (e.g., 'card', 'menu', 'search-form'). An Element is a part of a block that has no standalone meaning and is semantically tied to its block (e.g., 'card__title', 'menu__item', 'search-form__input'). A Modifier is a flag on a block or element that changes its appearance, behavior, or state (e.g., 'card--featured', 'menu__item--active', 'button--disabled'). The double underscore separates blocks from elements, and the double hyphen separates names from modifiers.

BEM's power lies in the constraints it imposes. First, every class is flat -- there are no nested selectors like .card .title, which eliminates specificity escalation. Second, classes are self-documenting: reading 'search-form__input--error' immediately tells you this is the input element of the search-form block in its error state. Third, blocks are independent and portable -- you can move a block from one page to another without its styles breaking because BEM classes do not depend on their DOM context.

In practice, BEM means you never style tag names or IDs, never nest selectors more than one level, and never use descendant combinators for styling. Every styled element gets its own flat BEM class. This feels verbose at first, especially compared to utility-first or nested SCSS approaches, but the tradeoff is predictability. On large codebases with many developers, BEM prevents the most common CSS maintenance problems: unexpected style inheritance, selector specificity conflicts, and the fear of modifying shared styles.

BEM works exceptionally well with CSS preprocessors like SASS/SCSS. The parent selector (&) lets you write nested BEM syntax that compiles to flat selectors. For example, &__title inside .card {} compiles to .card__title. Many teams combine BEM with ITCSS (Inverted Triangle CSS) for file organization, or with component-based architectures where each component has its own BEM-scoped stylesheet. BEM also integrates naturally with component frameworks: a React component named SearchForm would use classes like search-form, search-form__input, and search-form__button.

codeExamples.title: BEM naming: Block, Element, and Modifier
codeExamples.code:
<!-- BLOCK: Standalone component -->
<form class="search-form">
  <!-- ELEMENT: Part of the block (block__element) -->
  <input class="search-form__input" type="text" placeholder="Search..." />
  <button class="search-form__button" type="submit">Search</button>
</form>

<!-- MODIFIER on block: Variant of the whole component -->
<form class="search-form search-form--dark">
  <input class="search-form__input" type="text" />
  <button class="search-form__button" type="submit">Search</button>
</form>

<!-- MODIFIER on element: Variant of a single part -->
<form class="search-form">
  <input class="search-form__input search-form__input--large" type="text" />
  <button class="search-form__button search-form__button--disabled" type="submit">Search</button>
</form>

<style>
  /* Block */
  .search-form {
    display: flex;
    gap: 8px;
    padding: 16px;
    background: #fff;
    border: 1px solid #e5e7eb;
    border-radius: 8px;
  }

  /* Elements */
  .search-form__input {
    flex: 1;
    padding: 8px 12px;
    border: 1px solid #d1d5db;
    border-radius: 4px;
    font-size: 14px;
  }

  .search-form__button {
    padding: 8px 16px;
    background: #3b82f6;
    color: #fff;
    border: none;
    border-radius: 4px;
    cursor: pointer;
  }

  /* Block modifier */
  .search-form--dark {
    background: #1f2937;
    border-color: #374151;
  }

  /* Element modifiers */
  .search-form__input--large {
    padding: 12px 16px;
    font-size: 18px;
  }

  .search-form__button--disabled {
    opacity: 0.5;
    cursor: not-allowed;
  }
</style>
codeExamples.explanation: Blocks are standalone components. Elements are parts of blocks, connected by double underscores. Modifiers change appearance or state, connected by double hyphens. Each class is flat (no nesting), self-documenting, and independent of DOM hierarchy.

codeExamples.title: BEM with SCSS using the parent selector
codeExamples.explanation: SCSS parent selector (&) makes BEM convenient to write while compiling to flat selectors. &__title inside .card compiles to .card__title. &--featured compiles to .card--featured. This keeps the source code organized by component while maintaining BEM's flat specificity.

codeExamples.title: Real-world BEM: Navigation component
codeExamples.code:
• Home
  • Products
  • About
codeExamples.explanation: A real navigation component shows BEM's strengths: every piece has a clear, unique class name. main-nav__link--active is immediately readable as 'the active link inside the main-nav block'. The component is entirely self-contained and can be moved anywhere without style conflicts.

codeExamples.title: BEM anti-patterns and correct alternatives
codeExamples.explanation: Never chain elements (block__el1__el2). Elements always belong to the block directly. Never qualify with tag names. Always include the base class alongside any modifier class. Use key-value modifiers when there are multiple options for the same property.

notes: BEM class names can feel verbose, but they are explicit and self-documenting. In production, CSS minification and gzip compression eliminate any size concerns.
Blocks should never depend on other blocks. If a card looks different inside a sidebar, add a modifier to the card (card--sidebar) rather than writing .sidebar .card.
Elements cannot exist outside their block. If an element makes sense independently, it should be its own block.
BEM does not dictate file structure, but a common pattern is one file per block: card.css contains all .card, .card__*, and .card--* rules.
commonPitfalls: Chaining elements with double underscores (block__element__subelement). Elements always belong directly to the block, never to other elements.
Applying a modifier class without the base class. Always include both: class='button button--primary', not just class='button--primary'.
Overusing modifiers when a new block is more appropriate. If a modified version shares almost no styles with the original, create a separate block.
Mixing BEM with deep descendant selectors (.sidebar .card__title), which defeats BEM's flat specificity advantage.
interviewTips: Define Block, Element, and Modifier with examples. Block = standalone component (menu), Element = part of block (menu__item), Modifier = variant (menu__item--active).
Explain why BEM uses flat selectors: to avoid specificity wars, make styles predictable, and enable safe refactoring on large teams.
Compare BEM to other methodologies: OOCSS (separates structure from skin), SMACSS (categorizes rules), Atomic CSS (single-purpose classes). BEM is the most widely adopted.
Mention that BEM pairs well with component architectures (React, Vue) where each component maps to a BEM block.

exercises.title: Convert to BEM Naming
exercises.description: Rewrite the following CSS that uses non-BEM selectors (nested, tag-qualified, IDs) into proper BEM methodology. Create a product card component with a header, image, title, price (with a sale variant), description, and two action buttons (primary and secondary). Only use flat BEM class selectors.
exercises.starterCode:
Product Name

$29.99

Great product.
exercises.solution:
Product Name

$29.99

Great product.
hints: The block is 'product-card'. All parts become elements: product-card__header, product-card__image, product-card__title, etc.
Sale and primary are modifiers: product-card__price--sale, product-card__action--primary. Always include the base class alongside the modifier.

exercises.title: Build a BEM Notification Component
exercises.description: Create a complete notification component using BEM methodology. The notification block should support 4 modifier types (success, error, warning, info), have elements for icon, title, message, and a close button. Write both the HTML (showing all 4 variants) and the CSS. Use only flat BEM selectors -- no nesting, no tag selectors, no IDs.
exercises.starterCode:
<!-- Create 4 notification variants using BEM -->
<style>
  /* Write BEM styles for the notification component */
</style>

<!-- Success notification -->
<!-- Error notification -->
<!-- Warning notification -->
<!-- Info notification -->
exercises.solution:
✔

Success

Your changes have been saved successfully.

✖

Error

Something went wrong. Please try again.

⚠

Warning

Your session will expire in 5 minutes.

ℹ

Information

A new version is available for download.
hints: The block is 'notification'. Elements: notification__icon, notification__content, notification__title, notification__message, notification__close.
Block modifiers for types: notification--success, notification--error, notification--warning, notification--info. Each modifier changes colors only.

content.explanation:
CSS Modules are a CSS file organization approach where class names are scoped locally to the component that imports them, preventing naming collisions by default. Unlike global CSS where every class name is available everywhere (and can conflict with any other class), CSS Modules generate unique, hash-based class names at build time. When you write .title in a CSS Module, the build tool transforms it into something like .ProductCard_title_x7d3k, guaranteeing uniqueness across your entire application without requiring naming conventions like BEM.

The way CSS Modules work is straightforward. You create a CSS file with a special naming convention (usually component.module.css), write normal CSS classes in it, and import the file as a JavaScript object in your component. The import returns an object where keys are your original class names and values are the generated unique class names. You then use these values as className attributes. For example, importing styles from './Card.module.css' and using styles.title gives you the hashed class name. The actual HTML will have the unique class, and the generated CSS will use matching selectors.

Composition is a powerful CSS Modules feature that allows one class to inherit styles from another. Using the composes keyword, you can compose from the same file or from other CSS Module files. This is similar to @extend in SASS but happens at the class name level rather than the selector level. When you write .primaryButton { composes: button; background: blue; }, the element receives both class names in the HTML, and each class retains its own flat selector in the CSS. This avoids the specificity problems that SASS @extend can create.

CSS Modules integrate with all major build tools and frameworks. In Create React App and Vite, files ending in .module.css are automatically treated as CSS Modules. In webpack, the css-loader with modules: true enables CSS Modules for configured file patterns. Next.js supports CSS Modules out of the box. The build tool handles generating unique class names, creating the mapping object, and outputting the transformed CSS. In development, class names are often readable (Component_className_hash), while in production they are fully minified.

CSS Modules strike a balance between the familiarity of writing normal CSS and the safety of scoped styles. You get the full power of CSS (media queries, pseudo-selectors, animations, custom properties) without learning a new syntax or runtime library. The generated styles are static CSS with zero runtime cost, unlike CSS-in-JS solutions. The main limitation is that CSS Modules only scope class names and animation names -- tag selectors, ID selectors, and global styles are not scoped. For truly global styles, CSS Modules provide the :global() escape hatch.

codeExamples.title: Basic CSS Modules usage with React
codeExamples.explanation: You write plain CSS with simple class names. The import gives you a styles object mapping original names to unique hashed names. Using styles.title in JSX outputs the hashed class in the DOM. No naming collisions are possible because every class name is guaranteed unique.

codeExamples.title: Composition: sharing styles between classes
codeExamples.explanation: The composes keyword lets one class inherit styles from another. Unlike SASS @extend which duplicates selectors, composes applies multiple class names to the element. The element gets both the base class and the variant class, keeping specificity flat and styles composable.

codeExamples.title: Global styles and the :global escape hatch
codeExamples.explanation: By default, all class names in a .module.css file are locally scoped. Use :global() to opt specific classes out of scoping. This is useful for utility classes, third-party library overrides, and theme classes. For global styles like resets, use a regular .css file imported without the module convention.

codeExamples.title: Dynamic class names and conditional styling
codeExamples.explanation: Dynamic class names use bracket notation (styles[type]) to select classes based on props. Conditional classes can be applied via template literals, array filtering, or the clsx/classnames utility library. The clsx library is the recommended approach for complex conditional class logic in React.

notes: CSS Modules only scope class names and @keyframes animation names. Tag selectors (h1, p, div), attribute selectors, and ID selectors remain global.
In Vite and Create React App, files must end with .module.css to be treated as CSS Modules. Regular .css files are global.
CSS Modules have zero runtime cost. All class name transformations happen at build time, producing standard CSS files.
For TypeScript projects, use 'typed-css-modules' or 'typescript-plugin-css-modules' to get type checking and autocomplete for CSS Module imports.
commonPitfalls: Trying to use string class names directly instead of the styles object. Writing className='title' instead of className={styles.title} applies a non-existent global class.
Assuming tag selectors are scoped. Writing 'h1 { color: red; }' in a module file affects ALL h1 elements globally.
Not using the .module.css extension, which causes the file to be treated as global CSS without any scoping.
Over-using :global() which defeats the purpose of CSS Modules. Keep global styles in dedicated global files.
interviewTips: Explain the build-time transformation: .title becomes .Component_title_hash, guaranteeing uniqueness without runtime cost.
Compare CSS Modules to BEM (manual naming discipline vs automated scoping) and CSS-in-JS (build-time vs runtime style generation).
Mention composition as a key advantage over plain CSS, allowing style reuse without selector duplication or specificity issues.
Discuss the :global() escape hatch and when it is needed: third-party library overrides, theme classes, and utility classes.

exercises.title: Create a CSS Module Component
exercises.description: Write a CSS Module file (Badge.module.css) and a React component (Badge.jsx) for a badge/tag component. The badge should have a base style and three color variants: 'default' (gray), 'success' (green), and 'danger' (red). Use the composes keyword to share base styles across variants. The component should accept a 'variant' prop to select the color.
hints: Create a .base class with shared padding, font-size, and border-radius. Each variant uses 'composes: base' to inherit these styles.
Use bracket notation styles[variant] to dynamically select the class based on the variant prop.

exercises.title: CSS Module with Conditional Classes
exercises.description: Create a CSS Module for a TextInput component that supports: a base input style, a 'focused' state with a blue border, an 'error' state with a red border and error message, a 'disabled' state with reduced opacity, and a label element. Write the CSS Module with all states and the React component that conditionally applies classes based on props: error (string), disabled (boolean), and label (string). Use the clsx pattern (array filter) for combining classes.
hints: Use the array.filter(Boolean).join(' ') pattern to conditionally combine class names. false/undefined values are filtered out.
Manage focus state with useState and onFocus/onBlur handlers. Apply the focused class only when focused AND there is no error.

content.explanation:
CSS-in-JS is an approach where CSS styles are written directly in JavaScript files, co-located with the components they style. Instead of separate .css files, styles are defined as JavaScript objects or tagged template literals, and the library handles generating unique class names and injecting the CSS into the document. This approach gained popularity with React because it solves scoping, dead code elimination, and dynamic styling challenges that are difficult with traditional CSS.

The most well-known CSS-in-JS library is styled-components, which uses tagged template literals to create React components with embedded styles. When you write const Button = styled.button`background: blue;`, styled-components creates a React component that renders a <button> with a generated unique class name and the corresponding CSS injected into a <style> tag. The key innovation is that these styles can reference JavaScript variables and component props, enabling truly dynamic styling that would require inline styles or complex class toggling in traditional CSS.

Runtime CSS-in-JS libraries (styled-components, Emotion, JSS) generate and inject CSS at runtime in the browser. When a component renders, the library serializes the styles, generates a hash-based class name, and inserts a <style> tag into the document head. This enables powerful features like prop-based dynamic styles, theming via React context, automatic vendor prefixing, and automatic critical CSS extraction. However, the runtime cost is the main tradeoff: serializing styles, computing hashes, and injecting CSS adds overhead to every render, which can impact performance in large applications.

Zero-runtime CSS-in-JS libraries (Linaria, vanilla-extract, Panda CSS, StyleX) extract styles at build time and produce static CSS files with zero JavaScript runtime. You write styles using a similar API, but a compiler processes them during the build step, replacing the style definitions with generated class names and outputting standard CSS files. This gives you the developer experience of CSS-in-JS (co-location, type safety, variables) without any runtime performance cost. The tradeoff is that dynamic styles based on runtime values require CSS custom properties or predefined variants rather than arbitrary expressions.

The CSS-in-JS landscape has evolved significantly. The original premise -- that co-locating styles with components improves maintainability -- remains valid. However, the industry trend has shifted away from runtime solutions toward zero-runtime alternatives and CSS Modules. The React team's introduction of Server Components made runtime CSS-in-JS libraries incompatible with streaming SSR, accelerating this shift. Today, the recommended approaches for new React projects are CSS Modules, Tailwind CSS, or zero-runtime CSS-in-JS. Runtime libraries like styled-components remain widely used in existing codebases and are not going away, but new projects increasingly choose alternatives with lower performance overhead.

codeExamples.title: Styled-components: Basic usage and props
codeExamples.explanation: Styled-components uses tagged template literals to create React components with embedded styles. Props are available inside the template via interpolation functions. The styled() function extends existing styled components. Each component gets a unique class name automatically.

codeExamples.title: Theming with CSS-in-JS
codeExamples.explanation: ThemeProvider injects a theme object via React Context that all styled components can access via props.theme. Switching themes re-renders all themed components with new values. The css helper creates reusable style mixins. Destructuring ({ theme }) in template functions is a common shorthand.

codeExamples.title: Zero-runtime CSS-in-JS with vanilla-extract
codeExamples.explanation: vanilla-extract compiles .css.ts files at build time, producing standard CSS files. The API uses JavaScript objects instead of template literals, providing full TypeScript type checking. Theme tokens become CSS custom properties. The output is identical to CSS Modules: hashed class names with zero runtime JavaScript.

codeExamples.title: Runtime vs zero-runtime comparison
codeExamples.code:
/*
  RUNTIME CSS-in-JS (styled-components, Emotion)
  -----------------------------------------------
  Pros:
  + Fully dynamic styles based on any runtime value
  + Automatic critical CSS (only used styles are injected)
  + Familiar template literal syntax
  + Powerful theming via React Context

  Cons:
  - Runtime overhead: serialization + hashing + injection per render
  - Larger bundle size (library code: ~12-15KB gzipped)
  - Incompatible with React Server Components / streaming SSR
  - Can cause style recalculation on every render

  When to use:
  - Existing projects already using styled-components
  - Highly dynamic UIs where styles depend on runtime data
  - Prototyping and small projects where DX matters most
*/

// styled-components (runtime)
const Box = styled.div`
  /* This runs in the browser on every render */
  width: ${props => props.width}px;
  background: ${props => props.color};
`;

/*
  ZERO-RUNTIME CSS-in-JS (vanilla-extract, Linaria, Panda CSS)
  -----------------------------------------------------------
  Pros:
  + Zero runtime cost (static CSS output)
  + Full TypeScript type safety
  + Compatible with Server Components and streaming SSR
  + Smaller bundle size (no library runtime)

  Cons:
  - Cannot use arbitrary runtime values in styles
  - Requires build tool integration
  - Dynamic styles need CSS custom properties or predefined variants
  - Slightly more complex setup

  When to use:
  - New projects prioritizing performance
  - Server Component architectures (Next.js App Router)
  - Large applications where runtime CSS cost accumulates
*/

// vanilla-extract (zero-runtime)
// Dynamic values via CSS custom properties
export const box = style({
  width: 'var(--box-width)',
  background: 'var(--box-color)',
});

// In component: set CSS variables for dynamic values
// <div className={box} style={{ '--box-width': '200px', '--box-color': 'blue' }} />
codeExamples.explanation: Runtime libraries generate CSS in the browser, enabling fully dynamic styles but adding performance overhead. Zero-runtime libraries extract CSS at build time, producing static files with no runtime cost. Dynamic values in zero-runtime solutions use CSS custom properties set via inline style attributes.

notes: The styled-components library generates one class per unique set of interpolated values. If props change, a new class is generated and the old one is garbage collected.
Emotion offers both a styled API (like styled-components) and a css prop API that applies styles directly without creating wrapper components.
CSS custom properties (var(--name)) bridge the gap between static CSS and dynamic values -- they can be set from JavaScript and read by CSS without runtime CSS-in-JS.
The trend in 2025-2026 is toward zero-runtime solutions (vanilla-extract, Panda CSS) or utility-first CSS (Tailwind) over runtime CSS-in-JS for new projects.
commonPitfalls: Creating styled components inside render functions, which generates new component references every render and destroys React's reconciliation (always define styled components outside the component).
Over-using dynamic interpolations when static variants would suffice. Every unique interpolation result generates a new CSS class at runtime.
Not considering Server Component compatibility. Runtime CSS-in-JS libraries like styled-components do not work in React Server Components.
Using CSS-in-JS for styles that never change (static layouts, typography). These are better served by CSS Modules or a global stylesheet with no runtime cost.
interviewTips: Explain the core mechanism: CSS is generated from JavaScript, class names are hashed for uniqueness, and styles are injected into the DOM via <style> tags.
Distinguish runtime (styled-components, Emotion) from zero-runtime (vanilla-extract, Linaria). Explain the performance tradeoff: DX and dynamism vs runtime cost.
Discuss the React Server Components impact: runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible because it requires client-side JavaScript to generate styles.
Compare to alternatives: CSS Modules (build-time scoping, standard CSS), Tailwind (utility classes, no custom CSS), plain CSS with custom properties (simplest, most performant).

exercises.title: Build a Styled Component System
exercises.description: Using the styled-components pattern (you may write pseudocode or use template literal syntax), create a small component system with: a Container component (max-width, centered), a Heading that accepts a 'level' prop for different sizes, a Button with 'primary', 'secondary', and 'danger' variants via a 'variant' prop, and a Flex component that accepts 'direction', 'gap', and 'align' props. Show how each component would be used.
hints: Use props interpolation inside template literals: ${(props) => props.value}. Store variant mappings in objects outside the styled call for cleanliness.
The attrs method lets you set default HTML attributes and dynamic element types (like rendering as h1, h2, or h3 based on a 'level' prop).

exercises.title: Compare CSS-in-JS Approaches
exercises.description: Write the same Card component in three different approaches: (1) plain CSS with a separate stylesheet, (2) CSS Modules, and (3) styled-components. The card has a title, content area, and a footer with a button. Include a 'highlighted' variant that adds a blue left border. Show the CSS/JS for each approach and explain the tradeoffs in comments.
hints: For plain CSS, use BEM naming. For CSS Modules, use simple names (they are auto-scoped). For styled-components, create one styled component per element.
Focus on the tradeoffs: plain CSS has no tooling requirements but global scope. CSS Modules add scoping with no runtime cost. styled-components add full dynamism but with runtime overhead.

content.explanation:
Design tokens are the atomic, reusable values that define a design system's visual language. They are named entities that store design decisions such as colors, typography, spacing, border radii, shadows, and animation timings. Instead of hardcoding hex colors and pixel values throughout your CSS, you define tokens like --color-primary, --space-md, and --radius-lg, then reference them everywhere. When the brand color changes, you update one token and every component updates automatically. Design tokens are the single source of truth that bridges design tools, CSS, and even native platforms.

CSS custom properties (CSS variables) are the native web implementation of design tokens. Declared with the -- prefix and accessed via var(), they cascade through the DOM tree like regular CSS properties, making them ideal for theming. You can define tokens on :root for global scope, override them on specific elements or classes for local customization, and change them with JavaScript for runtime theme switching. Unlike preprocessor variables (SASS $variables), CSS custom properties are live values that exist at runtime and can be modified dynamically without recompilation.

A well-structured token system follows a hierarchy with three levels. Primitive tokens (also called global or base tokens) are raw values with abstract names: --color-blue-500, --font-size-16, --space-4. Semantic tokens (also called alias tokens) map primitives to purposes: --color-text-primary, --color-bg-surface, --button-padding. Component tokens are specific to individual components: --card-border-radius, --input-height, --nav-bg. This layered approach means changing the meaning of 'primary color' updates a single semantic token, while all components using that token automatically update.

Theming systems are built on top of design tokens by swapping token values based on context. The most common pattern is dark/light mode: you define a set of semantic tokens, then override their values when a .dark-theme class is applied to the document. Because CSS custom properties cascade, overriding a token on a parent element changes its value for all descendants. This same mechanism supports multiple themes (brand themes, high contrast mode, compact/comfortable density), user-customized accent colors, and per-section theming within a single page.

Design tokens extend beyond CSS. Tools like Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, and Amazon Style Dictionary transform token definitions (typically stored as JSON or YAML) into platform-specific outputs: CSS custom properties for web, XML resources for Android, Swift constants for iOS, and SCSS variables for legacy systems. This multi-platform approach ensures visual consistency across web, mobile, and desktop applications. The token definition file becomes the single source of truth that design tools (Figma), development environments, and documentation systems all reference.

codeExamples.title: Design token hierarchy with CSS custom properties
codeExamples.explanation: Primitive tokens are raw design values (colors, sizes). Semantic tokens map primitives to purposes (text-primary, bg-surface, interactive). Components reference semantic tokens, never primitives directly. This indirection means changing the brand color or theme only requires updating semantic token mappings.

codeExamples.title: Dark mode theming with token overrides
codeExamples.explanation: The dark theme overrides semantic token values on the .dark-theme class. Because CSS custom properties cascade, all components inside .dark-theme automatically use the dark values without any component-level changes. The @media query approach applies the theme based on the user's OS setting.

codeExamples.title: Component-level tokens for customization
codeExamples.explanation: Component tokens are CSS custom properties scoped to a component that default to semantic tokens. Variants override specific component tokens. Parent elements can override component tokens for all descendants. Inline styles can set individual tokens for per-instance customization. This creates a powerful, flexible theming API.

codeExamples.title: Token definition file (JSON) for multi-platform output
codeExamples.explanation: Design tokens are defined in a platform-agnostic format (JSON/YAML). Build tools like Style Dictionary transform them into platform-specific outputs: CSS custom properties, JavaScript constants, Swift extensions, Android XML resources. One token file drives consistent styling across web, iOS, and Android.

notes: CSS custom properties are inherited by default, making them ideal for theming. A token set on a parent element is available to all descendants.
Use the var() fallback parameter for resilience: var(--color-primary, #3b82f6) provides a default if the custom property is not defined.
Token naming should be descriptive of purpose, not appearance. Use --color-interactive instead of --color-blue, because the interactive color might not always be blue.
Figma Variables (introduced in 2023) directly map to design tokens, enabling designers and developers to share the same token definitions.
commonPitfalls: Referencing primitive tokens directly in components (var(--color-blue-500)) instead of semantic tokens (var(--color-interactive)). This makes theming impossible because components are hardcoded to specific colors.
Creating too many tokens for values that are only used once. Tokens should represent shared design decisions, not every single CSS value.
Not providing fallback values in var() for tokens that might not be defined, which causes properties to be ignored entirely.
Naming tokens by appearance (--color-dark-text) instead of purpose (--color-text-primary), which breaks when themes change.
interviewTips: Define design tokens as named, platform-agnostic values that represent design decisions. Explain the primitive -> semantic -> component hierarchy.
Demonstrate theming: show how overriding semantic token values on a parent class (like .dark-theme) automatically updates all components.
Mention that CSS custom properties cascade, are runtime values, and can be modified with JavaScript -- unlike SASS variables which are compile-time only.
Discuss the tooling ecosystem: Style Dictionary for multi-platform output, Figma Variables for designer-developer handoff, and Tokens Studio for token management.

exercises.title: Build a Token-Based Theme System
exercises.description: Create a CSS design token system with: (1) primitive tokens for a color palette (blue, gray, red, green with 3 shades each), spacing (4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32px), and border radii (4, 8, 12px). (2) Semantic tokens mapping primitives to purposes (text, backgrounds, borders, interactive states). (3) A .dark-theme class that overrides semantic tokens for dark mode. (4) A simple card component and button that use only semantic tokens.
exercises.starterCode:
Light Theme Card

Card content
exercises.solution:
Token-Based Card

This card uses semantic tokens for all visual properties.
hints: Define primitives first (raw color values, spacing values), then create semantic tokens that reference primitives via var(--primitive-name).
The dark theme class only overrides semantic tokens. Component styles reference semantic tokens only, so they automatically adapt to any theme.

exercises.title: Create a Multi-Theme Token System
exercises.description: Build a token system that supports three themes: light (default), dark, and a custom brand theme (use orange as the primary color). Define component tokens for a button component that derive from semantic tokens. Create CSS for all three themes and a button with 'primary' and 'outline' variants. Include a JavaScript function that switches between themes by toggling classes on the document element.
hints: Define semantic tokens on :root for light theme, then override them in .dark-theme and .brand-theme classes with different values.
The setTheme function should first remove all theme classes, then add the appropriate one. Light theme is the default (no class needed).
